feed_card.headline: Most mammals get about 1 billion heartbeats in a lifetime, whether they live two years or seventy.

teasers: Why shrews and whales share one number
The math that predicts your heart rate
How humans broke the billion-beat pattern

sections.content: Most mammals get about 1 billion heartbeats in a lifetime, whether they live two years or seventy.
🎯 7 questions at the end, earn Elo

sections.content: Measured in years, lifespans look random. A shrew gets two, a cat fifteen, an elephant sixty-five. Now count the heartbeats those years contain. They come out almost the same, near a billion in every case. So the year, the unit we trust, hides what is going on underneath. A small animal is not short on time so much as quick to spend it. The shrew packs into one day about as much heart-work as we get through in a week.

items: At one beat every second without stopping, a billion beats would take 31 years and 8 months.
A typical adult passes their billionth heartbeat at around age 27.
An elephant spends its whole billion over about 63 years; a mouse burns through it in three.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><text x='200' y='52' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='600' opacity='0.75' text-anchor='middle'>One human life</text><rect x='40' y='140' width='320' height='22' rx='11' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.4'/><rect x='40' y='140' width='102' height='22' rx='11' fill='var(--accent)' opacity='0.22'/><line x1='142' y1='116' x2='142' y2='186' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linecap='round'/><text x='142' y='108' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='15' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>1 billionth beat</text><text x='142' y='204' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.7' text-anchor='middle'>age 27</text><text x='251' y='132' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.6' text-anchor='middle'>beyond the budget</text><text x='42' y='204' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.6' text-anchor='start'>birth</text><text x='358' y='204' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.6' text-anchor='end'>~80 yrs</text></svg>

sections.content: There is no clever measurement behind this. You take an animal's resting pulse, you take how long it lives, you multiply. People have been writing both numbers down for over a century, from zoo logs to field studies. The catch is that you cannot see it in any single animal; a mouse is just a mouse with a fast heart and a short life. It shows up only when you line up species of wildly different sizes and watch their totals come to rest in the same place.

content.body: In 1883 the German physiologist Max Rubner offered a tidy reason big animals burn energy slowly: they shed heat through their skin, so metabolism should track surface area and scale as mass to the two-thirds power. The logic was clean and it held the field for fifty years. Then in 1932 Max Kleiber, a Swiss chemist measuring oxygen use in animals from rats to oxen at a California agricultural station, found his data would not sit on Rubner's line. Metabolism scaled as mass to the three-quarter power, and heart rate ran as its mirror image, falling as mass to the negative one-quarter power, which is why doubling an animal's weight drops its pulse by about sixteen percent. Why three-quarters and not two-thirds, nobody could say, and the exponent went unexplained for sixty-five years. In 1997 the physicist Geoffrey West, working with biologists James Brown and Brian Enquist, pulled the number out of the body's plumbing: blood vessels branch like a fractal tree that fills the body and ends in capillaries of one fixed size, and that single constraint forces the three-quarter law. The same year the cardiologist Herbert Levine multiplied heart rate by lifespan across fifteen species, watched the products gather near a billion, and gave the pattern its name.

key_figures.name: Max Kleiber
key_figures.one_line: In 1932 his measurements showed animal metabolism scaling at three-quarters of body mass, not the two-thirds everyone had assumed.

key_figures.name: Geoffrey West
key_figures.one_line: Trained as a physicist, he showed in 1997 that the three-quarter exponent falls straight out of the fractal way blood vessels branch.

key_figures.name: Herbert J. Levine
key_figures.one_line: The cardiologist who brought the billion-beat budget into medicine, plotting heart rate against lifespan across fifteen species in 1997.

content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><g stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' stroke-linecap='round' opacity='0.5'><line x1='60' y1='240' x2='370' y2='240'/><line x1='60' y1='240' x2='60' y2='40'/></g><line x1='80' y1='220' x2='350' y2='60' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linecap='round'/><g fill='currentColor'><circle cx='95' cy='213' r='3.5'/><circle cx='135' cy='185' r='3.5'/><circle cx='175' cy='167' r='3.5'/><circle cx='210' cy='140' r='3.5'/><circle cx='245' cy='125' r='3.5'/><circle cx='285' cy='97' r='3.5'/><circle cx='325' cy='77' r='3.5'/></g><g fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.7'><text x='215' y='270' text-anchor='middle'>Body mass (log)</text><text x='22' y='140' text-anchor='middle' transform='rotate(-90 22 140)'>Metabolic rate (log)</text></g><text x='350' y='52' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='15' font-weight='700' text-anchor='end'>slope = 3/4</text></svg>

content.title: Modern humans live past the biological budget
content.body: A human heart at seventy beats a minute over eighty years runs up about three billion beats, three times what most mammals get. The hearts themselves are nothing special; at 70 bpm we sit right where the formula puts an animal our size. What moved was lifespan. Before antibiotics, clean water, and safe childbirth, people typically died somewhere near thirty or forty, squarely on the universal billion. We stopped dying when biology used to kill us, and the meter kept running.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><g stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' stroke-linecap='round' opacity='0.5'><line x1='60' y1='250' x2='370' y2='250'/><line x1='60' y1='250' x2='60' y2='40'/></g><line x1='70' y1='185' x2='360' y2='185' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5' opacity='0.4' stroke-dasharray='6 6'/><text x='70' y='178' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.65' text-anchor='start'>~1B budget</text><g fill='currentColor' opacity='0.85'><circle cx='110' cy='188' r='3.5'/><circle cx='175' cy='183' r='3.5'/><circle cx='245' cy='186' r='3.5'/><circle cx='320' cy='182' r='3.5'/></g><circle cx='210' cy='95' r='5.5' fill='var(--accent)'/><text x='210' y='82' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='15' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>Humans ~3B</text><g fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.7'><text x='215' y='278' text-anchor='middle'>Mammals by size</text><text x='22' y='150' text-anchor='middle' transform='rotate(-90 22 150)'>Lifetime beats</text></g></svg>

content.title: Other primates beat the budget too, with no medicine to help
content.body: Humans are not the only primates over the line. A 2023 study in Montpellier put the gray mouse lemur at about three billion lifetime beats, a sixty-gram primate that lives a dozen years, three times what a rodent its size would spend. That high count, the study found, is what sets primates apart from other mammals their size, whatever the body weight. The advantage looks built into primate biology, not borrowed from medicine, though why has not been pinned down.
content.image_caption: The gray mouse lemur, a sixty-gram primate that runs up roughly three billion lifetime beats.
content.image_attribution: Charles J. Sharp, "Gray Mouse Lemur 1", CC BY-SA 3.0 (Wikimedia Commons)

content.title: Inside a single species, the rule turns over
content.body: Across species, bigger means longer-lived. Within a species it reverses. A Great Dane is old at seven or eight; a Chihuahua often passes fifteen while weighing thirty times less. The same shows up in lab mice, where the larger strains die younger and extra growth hormone shortens life. The billion-beat rule describes the differences evolution built between species, not how size plays out among individuals of one.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><text x='200' y='44' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='600' opacity='0.75' text-anchor='middle'>Within one species</text><line x1='60' y1='250' x2='340' y2='250' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.4' stroke-linecap='round'/><rect x='110' y='177' width='60' height='73' rx='12' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.5'/><text x='140' y='168' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>Great Dane</text><text x='140' y='219' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.8' text-anchor='middle'>7-8 yr</text><text x='140' y='270' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.6' text-anchor='middle'>~30 kg</text><rect x='230' y='95' width='60' height='155' rx='12' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='260' y='86' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>Chihuahua</text><text x='260' y='178' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>15+ yr</text><text x='260' y='270' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.6' text-anchor='middle'>~2 kg</text></svg>

sections.content: All of this points to a question biology still has not answered. Heart muscle seems to fail after roughly a set number of contractions, no matter how many years that takes, as though the cell keeps count of beats and ignores the calendar. The usual suspects in aging, worn telomeres, oxidative damage, tiring mitochondria, all run faster in tissue with a faster metabolism. Whatever is wearing out seems tied to the pace of living, not the passage of time. That is why living longer has mostly meant keeping the heart going longer: more years, the same rate of wear. Getting the cell to spend slower is the hard part, and nobody has done it.

content.question: What stays roughly constant across mammals, from shrew to whale?
options: Total heartbeats over a lifetime
Resting heart rate
Lifespan in years
Heart size relative to the body
content.explanation: Heart rate and lifespan each vary enormously, but their product, the total beats in a life, lands near a billion for most mammals. Small animals spend the budget fast, large ones slowly.

content.question: Why do a mouse and an elephant end up near the same lifetime beat count?
options: They share a recent common ancestor with the trait
Their hearts are the same size relative to body mass
Evolution fixed lifespan and heart rate separately at matching values
A faster heart goes with a shorter life, and the two effects cancel
content.explanation: Body mass drives heart rate down and lifespan up at related rates set by the same scaling laws. Multiply the two and the size effect cancels, leaving a near-constant product.

content.question: Kleiber's 1932 data showed metabolism scales with body mass raised to which power?
options: One, so it scales linearly with mass
Two-thirds
Three-quarters
It does not depend on mass
content.explanation: Surface-area reasoning had predicted two-thirds. Kleiber's measurements landed on three-quarters, and the same exponent later turned up in plants and microbes, hinting at a deeper cause.

content.question: When a mammal's body mass doubles, its resting heart rate roughly...
options: doubles
drops by about 16 percent
halves
stays the same
content.explanation: Heart rate falls as mass to the negative one-quarter power. Two to the negative one-quarter is about 0.84, so doubling the mass shaves roughly sixteen percent off the rate.

content.question: Modern humans run up about three billion beats, three times the mammal budget. Why?
options: Our heart muscle uses a tougher protein than other mammals
Our resting heart rate is unusually slow for our size
Evolution selected our hearts for extra durability
Medicine and hygiene let us live well past the lifespan biology selected for
content.explanation: At about 70 bpm our hearts sit right where the formula puts an animal our size. What changed is survival: we no longer die young from infection, childbirth, or injury, so the meter keeps running.

content.question: Inside a single species, how does body size relate to lifespan?
options: Larger individuals tend to live shorter lives, reversing the cross-species pattern
It matches the cross-species pattern, so larger individuals live longer
There is no relationship between size and lifespan within a species
Only birth weight matters; adult size is irrelevant
content.explanation: Great Danes die years before Chihuahuas, and larger mouse strains die younger than small ones. The billion-beat rule describes differences evolution built between species, not how size affects an individual within one.

content.question: What did West, Brown, and Enquist propose in 1997 to explain the three-quarter exponent?
options: Heat loss through skin surface sets the rate
The branching geometry of blood vessels constrains the scaling
Random variation across many species averages to three-quarters
Cellular metabolism is intrinsically fractal
content.explanation: They modeled the circulatory system as a space-filling fractal network of branching tubes ending in fixed-size capillaries. That geometric constraint forces the three-quarter exponent, and the model has since been applied to plants and even cities.

content.myth: A faster heart rate means a less efficient cardiovascular system.
content.reality: A small body simply requires a fast heart; a shrew's thousand-beat pulse is what the physics of its size demands, and its heart is no less efficient than an elephant's.

content.myth: All mammals get exactly one billion heartbeats.
content.reality: Across non-human mammals the figure runs from roughly half a billion to two billion. One billion is the order of magnitude, not a fixed constant, and some primates clear it easily.

content.myth: Modern humans evolved more durable hearts than our ancestors.
content.reality: Heart muscle and resting rate are no different from those of prehistoric humans. What changed is how long we survive, not the hardware doing the beating.

content.label: Levine HJ. Rest heart rate and life expectancy. JACC 1997;30(4):1104-1106
West GB, Brown JH, Enquist BJ. A general model for the origin of allometric scaling laws in biology. Science 1997;276:122-126

DiFrancesco ML et al. Characterization of sinoatrial automaticity in Microcebus murinus to study the effect of aging on cardiac activity and the correlation with longevity. Scientific Reports 2023;13:3054
